Category: Technology & Telecommunications: Information Technology, Science & Technology, Software, Hardware, Telecom
Description: When developers unintentionally enable unsafe or unintended AI behaviors.
Prerequisites: A basic familiarity with software development concepts and a general awareness of how AI-powered applications are built and deployed is all that is required to get started with this course.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify common ways developers unintentionally introduce unsafe or unintended behaviours into AI-powered applications, and explain the underlying technical and design factors that contribute to model misuse. You will also apply practical strategies to recognise, prevent, and address these vulnerabilities when building or reviewing software that incorporates intelligent models.

lessons.Description: This lesson explores how developers can unintentionally build applications that allow unsafe or unintended behaviours to emerge from the AI systems they integrate. Even well-meaning developers can make mistakes during the design and deployment process, such as failing to set appropriate boundaries, overlooking edge cases, or misunderstanding how the underlying model responds to certain inputs. Through practical examples, you will learn to recognise the most common ways misuse can occur within real-world applications, and why these issues often go unnoticed until they cause harm. By the end of this lesson, you will have a clearer understanding of your responsibilities as a developer, and the steps you can take to build safer, more reliable AI-powered products.
lessons.content: When developers unintentionally enable unsafe or unintended AI behaviors.

slides.subtitle: When developers accidentally enable unsafe behaviours.
slides.slidecontent:
• Developers building applications sometimes configure AI systems in ways that create unexpected risks
• Not always intentional
• Poor setup choices can strip away important safety boundaries
• Real-world consequences for end users who interact with these applications
• A growing concern as AI tools become embedded in everyday products and services

slides.subtitle: What counts as model misuse?
slides.slidecontent:
• When an application allows a model to behave in ways the developer never intended or anticipated

• Gaps between what a developer builds and what users actually do with it

• Not always deliberate

• Includes cases where safety boundaries are bypassed through clever prompting or poor input handling

• Missing guardrails, vague instructions, or overly permissive configurations that open the door to harm

• A spectrum from minor unintended outputs to serious real-world consequences

slides.subtitle: Signs that something has gone wrong
slides.slidecontent:
• Outputs that contradict the application's stated purpose or audience
• Unexpectedly detailed instructions for harmful activities
• Sudden shifts in tone, persona, or apparent intent
• Responses that ignore or override the system prompt's restrictions
• Confidential prompt content leaked into user-facing replies

slides.subtitle: Signs that something has gone wrong
slides.slidecontent:
• Model outputs that ignore or contradict the instructions given in the system prompt
• Responses that reveal confidential prompt contents when users ask the right questions
• Sudden shifts in tone or persona mid-conversation
• The model agreeing to tasks it was explicitly told to refuse
• Outputs that escalate in harmful detail the more a user pushes back
• Consistent patterns of off-topic or nonsensical replies suggesting broken context handling

slides.subtitle: Warning signs to watch for now
slides.slidecontent:
• Outputs that ignore safety rules
• Unexpected role changes mid-session
• Refusals suddenly disappearing
• Sensitive data appearing unprompted
• Responses escalating in harm

slides.subtitle: Warning signs and red flags explained
slides.slidecontent:
• Outputs that ignore safety rules
• Unexpected role-playing responses
• Leaking private system instructions
• Refusing nothing, ever
• Confidently wrong answers

slides.subtitle: Safeguarding Your Application From the Start
slides.slidecontent:
• Validate all user inputs first
• Set strict output boundaries
• Log unusual model behaviour
• Test edge cases regularly
• Limit model permissions always

slides.subtitle: Core principles for responsible AI development
slides.slidecontent:
• Validate all user inputs first
• Test for unintended behaviours early
• Limit model permissions by default
• Review outputs before deployment
• Revisit safeguards regularly

scripts.script: Welcome to this lesson on model misuse in applications. Now, when we talk about misuse here, we are not necessarily talking about someone with bad intentions deliberately trying to cause harm. More often than not, the problem comes from developers who are simply building products and making configuration choices that, perhaps without realising it, end up removing important safety guardrails from the AI systems they are working with. Those guardrails exist for good reason, and when they are stripped away, even accidentally, real users interacting with those applications can be exposed to genuinely harmful outputs. And this is not a niche or theoretical concern. As AI tools become woven into everyday products and services, from customer support chatbots to educational platforms, the potential impact of these poor setup choices grows significantly. So throughout this lesson, we are going to look at how this kind of misuse happens, why it matters, and what responsible development should look like instead.

So what do we actually mean by model misuse? At its core, it's about the gap between what a developer intends their application to do and what it ends up doing in the real world. And here's the thing, it isn't always someone deliberately trying to cause harm. Sometimes it happens simply because the application wasn't built with enough care around how users might interact with it. That could mean missing guardrails, instructions that are too vague, or configurations that are far too permissive. It can also include situations where someone uses clever prompting to push the model past its safety boundaries, or where poor input handling leaves the door wide open to problems. And the consequences sit on a spectrum. At one end you might get slightly odd or unexpected outputs. At the other end, you're looking at genuinely serious real-world harm. So misuse isn't one single thing. It's a whole range of ways an application can go wrong.

So how do you actually spot when a model is behaving in a way it shouldn't? There are a few warning signs worth knowing. First, if the model's output seems completely at odds with what the application is supposed to do, that's a red flag. A children's educational tool producing adult content, for example, is an obvious mismatch. Similarly, if a model starts giving unusually detailed guidance on something dangerous or harmful, that's a serious concern. You might also notice a sudden change in how the model sounds, as though it has adopted a different personality or shifted its apparent purpose mid-conversation. Another sign is when the model seems to be ignoring the rules it was given in the system prompt, essentially overriding its own instructions. And finally, if the model starts revealing text that was clearly meant to be hidden from users, such as internal instructions, that suggests something has gone wrong at a fairly fundamental level.

So how do you actually spot when a model is behaving unsafely in a live application? There are a few warning signs worth knowing. First, if the model starts ignoring or contradicting the instructions you set up in the system prompt, that is a red flag. Similarly, if a user can simply ask the model to repeat its instructions and it obliges, your confidential prompt is no longer confidential. Watch out too for the model suddenly changing its tone or personality mid-conversation, or agreeing to do things it was specifically told to refuse. Another concerning pattern is when a user pushes back repeatedly and the model's responses actually become more harmful rather than holding firm. And finally, if you are seeing consistent nonsensical or completely off-topic replies, that often points to something going wrong with how the model is handling context. Recognising these signs early gives you a much better chance of catching problems before they affect real users.

So how do you actually spot a model that's starting to behave unsafely? There are a few warning signs worth keeping in your mind. First, if the model begins ignoring the safety guidelines it was clearly following before, that's a red flag. Similarly, watch out for the model suddenly acting as though it has a different identity or purpose than the one it started with. Another sign is when a model that was previously declining certain requests stops doing so without any obvious reason. You should also be alert if sensitive or private information starts appearing in responses when nobody asked for it. And finally, pay attention to whether the outputs are gradually becoming more harmful over the course of a conversation, even in small steps. None of these things on their own are definitive proof of misuse, but together they paint a picture that something has gone wrong and deserves a closer look.

So how do you actually spot a model that's behaving unsafely? There are a few warning signs worth knowing. First, if a model starts ignoring its own safety guidelines and producing harmful or restricted content, that's a serious red flag. Similarly, if it suddenly starts acting like a character or persona it shouldn't be playing, something has likely gone wrong. Another concern is when a model leaks its internal system instructions, the private setup instructions it's supposed to keep confidential. Then there's what some people call "never refusing" behaviour, where the model agrees to absolutely everything without any pushback, which suggests its guardrails have been removed or bypassed. And finally, watch out for confident but completely wrong answers, sometimes called hallucinations. The model sounds certain, but the information is simply incorrect. Recognising these patterns early is really important, because each one can indicate that the model is being misused or has been compromised in some way.

So, now we get to the practical side of things: what can you actually do to protect your application from misuse? The first habit to build is checking whatever a user sends before it ever reaches the model. Don't assume the input is safe or sensible. From there, you want to be clear about what the model is allowed to output, setting firm boundaries so it doesn't wander into territory it shouldn't. It's also really worth keeping an eye on how the model behaves over time, because unusual patterns in its responses can be an early warning sign that something isn't right. On top of that, make sure you're regularly testing the awkward, unexpected scenarios, the edge cases that real users will inevitably stumble into. And finally, keep the model's permissions as limited as possible. Only give it access to what it genuinely needs. These five practices together form a solid foundation for building responsibly from the very start.

So let's bring everything together with a few core principles to keep in mind as you build with AI models. First, always check what users are actually sending to your model before it gets processed. Don't assume the input is safe or sensible. Second, test your application early and often, specifically looking for ways the model might behave in ways you didn't intend. Third, be cautious about what your model is allowed to do by default. Start with the minimum permissions necessary and expand from there only when needed. Fourth, don't just trust that the model's output is fine because it looks reasonable. Review it before it goes anywhere near real users. And finally, this isn't a one-time job. The landscape changes, models get updated, and new risks emerge, so revisit your safeguards on a regular basis. Keep these principles close and you'll be in a much stronger position to develop AI applications responsibly.

Who is this for: Software developers, application developers, AI developers, product managers, software engineers, technical leads, development team leads
Team or Dept this is for: Engineering, Software Development, Product Development, IT, Technology teams
